items.subtopic: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia | INCIDENCE AND EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.title: Incidence and Demographics of CML
items.content: CML accounts for approximately 15% of all leukemia cases, with a slight male predominance (1.6:1). The median age at diagnosis is between 55–65 years, though it is uncommon in children, affecting only about 3% under the age of 20.
keyPoints: CML represents ~15% of all leukemia cases.
Male-to-female ratio is 1.6:1.
Median age at diagnosis is 55–65 years.
Only 3% of patients are younger than 20 years.
items.reference: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia · Chronic Myeloid Leukemia | INCIDENCE AND EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia | INCIDENCE AND EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.title: Impact of TKI Therapy on CML Prevalence and Mortality
items.content: The introduction of BCR::ABL1 tyrosine kinase inhibitors (TKIs) has significantly improved the prognosis for CML patients, reducing annual mortality from 10–20% to about 1–2%. This reduction in mortality is expected to increase the prevalence of CML, with estimates suggesting a plateau at ~450,000 cases in the United States by 2040.
keyPoints: Annual incidence of CML is 2 per 100,000 individuals.
Approximately 9000 new cases per year in the U.S.
Annual mortality reduced to about 1–2% with TKI therapy.
Estimated plateau prevalence of ~450,000 cases in the U.S. by 2040.
items.reference: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia · Chronic Myeloid Leukemia | INCIDENCE AND EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: Causative Factors and Risk Assessment for Chronic Myeloid Leukemia (CML)
items.content: Chronic myeloid leukemia is not associated with familial patterns or specific etiologic agents. There are no increased risks in monozygotic twins or relatives of CML patients, nor associations with common environmental exposures like benzene or insecticides. However, exposure to ionizing radiation can increase the risk of developing CML, which peaks 5–10 years post-exposure and is dose-dependent.
keyPoints: No familial association in CML development.
Risk not increased among monozygotic twins or relatives.
No link with benzene, toxins, fertilizers, insecticides, or viruses.
CML rarely secondary to alkylating agents/radiation therapy for other cancers.
Ionizing radiation exposure increases CML risk, peaking 5–10 years post-exposure.
Median time to CML development among atomic bomb survivors: 6.3 years.
No increased incidence of CML post-Chernobyl suggests higher doses needed.
items.reference: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia · ETIOLOGY

items.title: Radiation Exposure and Chronic Myeloid Leukemia (CML) Risk
items.content: Ionizing radiation exposure, such as from nuclear accidents or high-dose treatments, increases the risk of developing CML. This risk peaks 5–10 years after exposure and is related to the dose received. The median time for CML development post-exposure among atomic bomb survivors was 6.3 years. No increased incidence of CML was noted following the Chernobyl accident, indicating that higher radiation doses are necessary to elevate CML risk.
keyPoints: Ionizing radiation exposure increases CML risk.
Risk peaks 5–10 years after exposure and is dose-related.
Median time for CML development post-atomic bomb: 6.3 years.
No increased CML incidence post-Chernobyl, suggesting higher doses needed.
items.reference: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia · ETIOLOGY

items.title: BCR-ABL1 Translocation and Its Role in CML
items.content: The t(9;22)(q34.1;q11.2) translocation, present in >90% of classical CML cases, results from a balanced reciprocal translocation between chromosomes 9 and 22. This creates the BCR::ABL1 oncogene, leading to constitutive kinase activity that promotes proliferation and reduces apoptosis of CML cells.
keyPoints: t(9;22)(q34.1;q11.2) present in >90% of classical CML cases
BCR::ABL1 oncogene results from translocation between chromosomes 9 and 22
Constitutive kinase activity leads to increased proliferation and reduced apoptosis
items.reference: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia · PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 1)

items.title: Different BCR-ABL1 Isoforms and Their Clinical Implications
items.content: The breakpoint in the BCR gene determines the isoform of BCR::ABL1 produced. The e13a2 and e14a2 transcripts encode p210BCR::ABL1, while the e1a2 transcript from a more centromeric breakpoint encodes p190BCR::ABL1, associated with worse outcomes in Ph-positive CML.
keyPoints: e13a2 and e14a2 transcripts encode p210BCR::ABL1
e1a2 transcript encodes p190BCR::ABL1, linked to worse outcomes
Breakpoint location affects isoform type and clinical prognosis
items.reference: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia · PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 1)

items.title: Mechanisms of BCR-ABL1 Inhibition by TKIs
items.content: BCR::ABL1 TKIs, such as imatinib and ponatinib, inhibit the kinase activity by binding to the ATP-binding domain. Asciminib targets the ABL1 myristoyl pocket, restoring negative regulatory activity lost upon fusion with BCR.
keyPoints: TKIs bind to BCR::ABL1 ATP-binding domain
Asciminib targets ABL1 myristoyl pocket (STAMP)
Restores inhibitory activity lost in BCR-ABL1 fusion
items.reference: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia · PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 1)

items.title: BCR::ABL1 Rearrangement Prevalence and Insufficiency for CML
items.content: The BCR::ABL1 rearrangement is detected in up to 25% of normal adults and 5% of infants, yet does not cause overt CML in most cases. This suggests additional factors are necessary for the development of CML.
keyPoints: BCR::ABL1 detected in up to 25% of normal adults
Detected in 5% of infants but 0% of cord blood samples
CML develops in only 2 of 100,000 individuals annually
items.reference: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia · PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 2)

items.title: Molecular Events and Prognosis in Atypical CML
items.content: Atypical CML and related conditions often have distinct molecular profiles and poor prognosis, with median survival of ~2–3 years. Detection of specific mutations can guide therapy.
keyPoints: Patients with atypical CML do not respond to TKI therapy
Median survival of ~2–3 years for these patients
CSF3R mutations in 80% of chronic neutrophilic leukemia cases
SETBP1 mutations in 25% of atypical CML cases
SF3B1 mutations in MDS/MPN-RS-T associated with longer survival (7 years vs 3.3 years)
items.reference: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia · PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 2)

items.title: Resistance Mechanisms and TKI Development
items.content: BCR::ABL1 rearrangement induces genetic instability, leading to resistance mechanisms such as ABL1 kinase domain mutations. Third-generation TKIs are effective against resistant mutations like T315I.
keyPoints: ABL1 kinase domain mutations can confer resistance to imatinib and second-generation TKIs
Over 100 ABL1 kinase domain mutations described
Third-generation TKIs (ponatinib, asciminib, olverembatinib) effective against T315I mutation
items.reference: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia · PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 2)

items.title: Presentation of CML in Different Healthcare Settings
items.content: The presentation of Chronic Myeloid Leukemia (CML) varies based on healthcare access. In developed countries, routine screenings lead to early diagnosis with minimal symptoms like fatigue in 50-60% of patients. Conversely, limited healthcare access results in presentations with higher disease burden and more severe symptoms.
keyPoints: In developed regions, 50–60% diagnosed via routine blood tests with minimal symptoms such as fatigue.
Limited healthcare access leads to high CML burden: splenomegaly, anemia, abdominal pain, weight loss, fatigue.
Higher frequency of high-risk CML in areas with limited healthcare.
items.reference: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia · CLINICAL PRESENTATION (part 1)

items.title: Symptoms and Phases of CML
items.content: Most patients present during the indolent or chronic phase, often asymptomatic if diagnosed through screening. Common symptoms include fatigue and splenomegaly manifestations. Severe leukocytosis can lead to thrombotic events. Progression to accelerated or blastic phases introduces additional severe symptoms.
keyPoints: 90% present in the indolent/chronic phase, often asymptomatic if diagnosed via screening.
Common symptoms: fatigue, malaise, weight loss (high leukemia burden), early satiety, left upper quadrant pain/masses from splenomegaly.
Severe leukocytosis can cause thrombotic events like priapism, cardiovascular complications, myocardial infarction, venous thrombosis.
Accelerated/blastic phases: fever, weight loss, severe fatigue, bone/joint pain, bleeding/thrombotic events, infections.
items.reference: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia · CLINICAL PRESENTATION (part 1)

items.title: Physical and Hematologic Findings in CML
items.content: Splenomegaly is the most common physical finding, occurring in 20-70% of patients. Leukocytosis with a range of $10-500 	imes 10^{9}/L$ is typical in untreated CML. A high myeloid-to-erythroid ratio and increased reticulin fibrosis are noted, though the latter resolves with TKI therapy.
keyPoints: Splenomegaly occurs in 20–70% of patients.
Leukocytosis ranges from $10-500 	imes 10^{9}/L$ in untreated CML.
Peripheral blood shows left-shifted hematopoiesis, predominance of neutrophils, and presence of immature cells (bands, myelocytes, etc.).
High myeloid-to-erythroid ratio: 15–20:1; marrow blasts typically ≤5%.
Reticulin fibrosis common in 30–40%, resolves with TKI therapy.
items.reference: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia · CLINICAL PRESENTATION (part 1)

items.title: Importance of FISH and PCR in Monitoring CML
items.content: FISH and PCR are crucial for estimating the burden of Chronic Myeloid Leukemia (CML) during TKI therapy. They provide a convenient method by using peripheral blood samples, aiding in monitoring response to treatment.
keyPoints: FISH is used to quantify Ph-positive cells if replacing marrow cytogenetic analysis.
PCR at diagnosis documents BCR::ABL1 transcripts to avoid false impressions of complete molecular response.
10-15% of patients may develop chromosomal abnormalities in Ph-negative metaphases after TKI therapy, which FISH cannot detect unless pre-identified.
FISH and PCR can be falsely positive/negative due to technical issues; marrow analysis is essential for diagnosis.
Partial cytogenetic response: ≤35% Ph-positive metaphases or BCR::ABL1 IS ≤10%; Complete cytogenetic response: 0% Ph positivity, BCR::ABL1 IS ≤1% (MR2).
Major molecular response (MMR): BCR::ABL1 IS ≤0.1%, deep molecular response (DMR): MR4 (IS ≤0.01%) and MR4.5 (IS ≤0.0032%).
items.reference: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia · CLINICAL PRESENTATION (part 2)

items.title: Diagnostic and Monitoring Strategies for CML
items.content: Diagnosis of CML relies on marrow analysis with routine cytogenetics to confirm the presence of Ph chromosome, detect additional chromosomal abnormalities, and quantify marrow blasts and basophils. Monitoring involves correlating cytogenetic results with PCR values.
keyPoints: Marrow analysis is essential for confirming diagnosis due to potential false positives/negatives in FISH/PCR.
In 10% of patients, marrow blasts/basophils are significantly higher than peripheral blood, indicating poorer prognosis or disease transformation.
Cytogenetic response: ≤35% Ph-positive metaphases; PCR equivalent: BCR::ABL1 IS ≤10%.
Complete cytogenetic response: 0% Ph positivity; PCR equivalent: BCR::ABL1 IS ≤1% (MR2).
MMR: BCR::ABL1 IS ≤0.1%; DMR: MR4 (IS ≤0.01%) and MR4.5 (IS ≤0.0032%).
items.reference: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia · CLINICAL PRESENTATION (part 2)

items.title: Criteria for Accelerated-Phase CML
items.content: Accelerated-phase CML is characterized by specific hematologic and cytogenetic criteria, including increased blasts and basophils in peripheral blood or marrow. Despite improved survival rates with TKI therapy, these criteria remain crucial for distinguishing accelerated-phase from chronic-phase CML.
keyPoints: Criteria include 15% or more peripheral blasts, 30% or more peripheral blasts plus promyelocytes, 20% or more peripheral basophils, cytogenetic clonal evolution, and thrombocytopenia <100 × 10⁹/L (unrelated to therapy).
Historical median survival of accelerated-phase CML was <2 years.
Estimated 8-year survival rate with TKI therapy is 60–70% for de novo accelerated phase.
Median survival from chronic phase has improved to an estimated 3-year survival rate of 50% on TKI therapy.
WHO suggests eliminating accelerated-phase as a separate entity, but it should be maintained due to worse prognosis compared to high-risk CML.
items.reference: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia · CLINICAL PRESENTATION (part 3)

items.title: Diagnosis and Treatment of Blastic-Phase CML
items.content: Blastic-phase CML is defined by a significant increase in blasts, with distinct myeloid or lymphoid presentations. Accurate immunophenotypic diagnosis is essential for effective treatment, as lymphoid blastic phase responds well to anti-ALL-type chemotherapy combined with TKIs.
keyPoints: Defined by 30% or more peripheral or marrow blasts or sheets of blasts in extramedullary disease.
Commonly myeloid (60%) but can present erythroid, promyelocytic, monocytic, or megakaryocytic.
Lymphoid blastic phase occurs in about 25% of patients, with lymphoblasts expressing CD10, CD19, CD20, CD22, and often myeloid markers (50–80%).
Lymphoid blastic-phase CML responds to anti-ALL-type chemotherapy combined with TKIs, achieving a complete response rate of 70% and median survival of 3 years.
High rates of bridging to allogeneic HSCT and possible cure.
items.reference: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia · CLINICAL PRESENTATION (part 3)

items.title: Impact of TKI Therapy on Mortality Rates in CML
items.content: The introduction of tyrosine kinase inhibitors (TKIs) has significantly reduced the annual mortality rate in chronic myeloid leukemia (CML). Before TKIs, the annual mortality was 10% in the first two years and increased to 15-20% thereafter. With imatinib therapy, this rate decreased dramatically to 1-2% annually over a 20-year observation period.
keyPoints: Annual mortality pre-TKI: 10% (first 2 years), 15–20% thereafter
Imatinib reduces annual mortality to 1–2%
10-year survival rate with imatinib: ~85%
CML-specific survival rate at 10 years: 90%
items.reference: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia · PROGNOSIS AND CML COURSE

items.title: Transformation and Survival in CML with TKI Therapy
items.content: The risk of transformation to accelerated or blastic phases in CML has decreased significantly with TKI therapy. The 10-year incidence of blastic transformation is now 5-6%. Second-generation TKIs further reduce this risk compared to imatinib, especially in the first few years.
keyPoints: 10-year incidence of blastic transformation: 5–6%
Second-generation TKIs reduce early transformation risk (1–2%)
Rare sudden transformations (<1% annually) with continued therapy
items.reference: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia · PROGNOSIS AND CML COURSE

items.title: Prognostic Factors and Treatment Endpoints in the Era of TKI Therapy
items.content: In the era of TKI therapy, treatment-related prognostic factors have become more important than traditional pretreatment factors. Complete cytogenetic response (MR2) is crucial for survival improvement, while major molecular response (MMR) reduces relapse risk but not necessarily survival. Durable deep molecular response (DMR) can lead to treatment-free remission.
keyPoints: Complete cytogenetic response (MR2) linked to improved survival
Major molecular response (MMR) decreases relapse risk
Durable DMR for 2+ years: TFR rate of 50%
Durable DMR for 5+ years: TFR rate >80%
items.reference: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia · PROGNOSIS AND CML COURSE

items.title: Understanding TKI Potency and Generations in CML Treatment
items.content: The treatment landscape for chronic myeloid leukemia (CML) has evolved significantly with the approval of various tyrosine kinase inhibitors (TKIs). These drugs differ in potency and generation, impacting their clinical use.
keyPoints: Six oral BCR::ABL1 TKIs are approved: imatinib, nilotinib, dasatinib, bosutinib, ponatinib, asciminib.
Nilotinib is 30 times more potent than imatinib.
Dasatinib is 300 times more potent and bosutinib is 30–50 times more potent than imatinib.
Ponatinib inhibits VEGFR, associated with high hypertension incidence.
Second-generation TKIs: dasatinib, nilotinib, bosutinib; Third-generation TKIs: ponatinib, asciminib.
items.reference: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia · Chronic Phase Chronic Myeloid Leukemia

items.title: Mechanism and Side Effects of Ponatinib in CML Treatment
items.content: Ponatinib's unique mechanism includes inhibition of VEGFR, which contributes to its side effect profile, particularly hypertension.
keyPoints: Ponatinib is a third-generation TKI active against T315-mutated CML.
Inhibits vascular endothelial growth factor receptor (VEGFR).
Associated with high incidence of hypertension.
items.reference: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia · Chronic Phase Chronic Myeloid Leukemia

items.subtopic: ESTABLISHED AND EVOLVING THERAPEUTIC CONCEPTS IN CML (part 1)
items.title: Optimal Biologic Dose in TKI Therapy for CML
items.content: The concept of using an 'optimal biologic dose' (OBD) rather than the maximum tolerated dose (MTD) emerged to maintain efficacy while reducing long-term toxicities in chronic myeloid leukemia (CML) treatment with tyrosine kinase inhibitors (TKIs). This approach was initially based on clinical experience and is now supported by translational studies.
keyPoints: OBD aims to reduce toxicities observed with long-term TKI exposure while maintaining efficacy.
Dasatinib 50 mg daily demonstrated similar effectiveness but less toxicity compared to 100 mg daily as frontline therapy.
Dose adjustments after achieving a good molecular response can maintain efficacy.
items.reference: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia · ESTABLISHED AND EVOLVING THERAPEUTIC CONCEPTS IN CML (part 1)

items.subtopic: ESTABLISHED AND EVOLVING THERAPEUTIC CONCEPTS IN CML (part 1)
items.title: Frontline Therapy Options and Outcomes in CML
items.content: Imatinib, dasatinib, bosutinib, and nilotinib are all acceptable frontline therapies for CML. Long-term results with imatinib show high rates of major molecular response (MMR) and deep molecular response (DMR), alongside favorable survival outcomes.
keyPoints: 10-year cumulative rate of MMR with imatinib is 90%.
10-year cumulative rate of DMR is 80%.
10-year survival rate is 82%, with a relative survival rate of 92% compared to an age-matched population.
About 25% of patients switch to second-generation TKIs, with 10% due to resistance and 15% for other reasons such as adverse events.
items.reference: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia · ESTABLISHED AND EVOLVING THERAPEUTIC CONCEPTS IN CML (part 1)

items.subtopic: ESTABLISHED AND EVOLVING THERAPEUTIC CONCEPTS IN CML (part 1)
items.title: Goals of TKI Therapy in CML Beyond Survival
items.content: After achieving near-normal survival rates, the goals of TKI therapy in CML expanded to include curability, accessibility, and minimizing toxicities. This includes exploring treatment-free remission (TFR) and making therapies affordable.
keyPoints: Second goal: Assessing if CML is curable by stopping TKI therapy without recurrence after several years of durable deep molecular response (DMR).
Third goal: Making TKIs available and affordable to all patients, addressed with generic versions.
Fourth goal: Minimizing early and long-term toxicities through studies on OBDs.
items.reference: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia · ESTABLISHED AND EVOLVING THERAPEUTIC CONCEPTS IN CML (part 1)

items.subtopic: ESTABLISHED AND EVOLVING THERAPEUTIC CONCEPTS IN CML (part 2)
items.title: Choosing the Appropriate TKI for CML Based on Treatment Aims and Patient Factors
items.content: The selection of a tyrosine kinase inhibitor (TKI) for chronic myeloid leukemia (CML) therapy is influenced by treatment goals, patient age, cost considerations, and comorbidities. Survival outcomes are similar across all TKIs, but second-generation TKIs may be preferred for achieving treatment-free remission (TFR) or in high-risk cases.
keyPoints: Survival outcomes with any of the four TKIs are comparable.
Second-generation TKIs might be favored for TFR and high-risk chronic-phase CML.
Patient comorbidities influence TKI choice: avoid dasatinib with lung issues, nilotinib with pancreatitis or diabetes, bosutinib with liver/renal dysfunction.
items.reference: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia · ESTABLISHED AND EVOLVING THERAPEUTIC CONCEPTS IN CML (part 2)

items.subtopic: ESTABLISHED AND EVOLVING THERAPEUTIC CONCEPTS IN CML (part 2)
items.title: Frontline TKI Dosing and Comparative Efficacy of Asciminib
items.content: Standard frontline dosing for TKIs includes imatinib at 400 mg daily, dasatinib at 100 mg daily, bosutinib at 400 mg daily, and nilotinib at 300 mg twice daily. A trial showed asciminib had a higher major molecular response (MMR) compared to other TKIs, though its long-term benefits remain uncertain.
keyPoints: Frontline dosing: imatinib 400 mg/day; dasatinib 100 mg/day; bosutinib 400 mg/day; nilotinib 300 mg twice daily.
Asciminib showed a higher 12-month MMR (68%) compared to other TKIs (49%); p<.001.
The long-term impact of asciminib's higher MMR on survival, TFR, and side effects is not yet determined.
items.reference: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia · ESTABLISHED AND EVOLVING THERAPEUTIC CONCEPTS IN CML (part 2)

items.subtopic: ESTABLISHED AND EVOLVING THERAPEUTIC CONCEPTS IN CML (part 2)
items.title: Management of TKI Toxicities in CML
items.content: TKI toxicities can often be managed by dose reduction without compromising efficacy, especially if the patient is in good molecular response. Second-generation TKIs generally have fewer mild to moderate side effects compared to imatinib, but specific risks vary among them.
keyPoints: Dose reductions are viable for managing reversible toxicities while maintaining efficacy.
Common side effects of imatinib include fluid retention and skin rashes (10-20%).
Dasatinib 100 mg daily has higher rates of myelosuppression (20-30%) and pleural effusions (10-25%).
Nilotinib is associated with hyperglycemia (10-20%) and cardiovascular complications (25-35% over 10 years).
items.reference: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia · ESTABLISHED AND EVOLVING THERAPEUTIC CONCEPTS IN CML (part 2)

items.subtopic: ESTABLISHED AND EVOLVING THERAPEUTIC CONCEPTS IN CML (part 3)
items.title: Managing Toxicities in TKI Therapy for CML
items.content: When toxicities arise from TKI therapy, such as pleural effusions or vasospastic events, switching to a different TKI may be necessary. The dose of the new TKI does not need to be at the 'failure' level if the patient is already in good molecular response.
keyPoints: Recurrent pleural effusions most common with dasatinib; least with imatinib and nilotinib.
Vasospastic events more common with ponatinib and nilotinib; least with imatinib and bosutinib.
Pulmonary hypertension occurs in 1–2% with dasatinib, reversible with steroids and sildenafil.
Pancreatitis: 2% with nilotinib, 2–4% with ponatinib.
Neurologic problems are rare and reversible with TKI discontinuation.
Immunemediated events usually reversible with high-dose steroids (e.g., methylprednisolone 50 mg twice daily for 3–5 days).
Dose of new TKI can be lower if patient is in ≥MR2: dasatinib 20–50 mg, bosutinib 100–300 mg, nilotinib 200 mg or 150 mg BID, ponatinib 15–30 mg.
items.reference: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia · ESTABLISHED AND EVOLVING THERAPEUTIC CONCEPTS IN CML (part 3)

items.subtopic: ESTABLISHED AND EVOLVING THERAPEUTIC CONCEPTS IN CML (part 3)
items.title: Criteria and Considerations for TKI Discontinuation and Treatment-Free Remission (TFR) in CML
items.content: TKI discontinuation is possible after achieving deep molecular response (DMR) for over 2–3 years, with TFR rates of 40–60%. After 5+ years of DMR, TFR rates can reach 80%+. Monitoring post-discontinuation involves regular molecular assessments.
keyPoints: Discontinuation after >2–3 years of DMR results in TFR rates of 40–60%.
After 5+ years of DMR, TFR rates are 80%+.
Suggested conditions for TFR: low/intermediate Sokal risk, quantifiable BCR::ABL1 transcripts, long-term TKI therapy (5+ years), documented DMR >2–5 years.
Monitoring post-discontinuation: every 1.5–2 months first 6–12 months, every 2–3 months second year, then every 3–6 months for up to 6–8 years.
TKIs should be restarted if BCR::ABL1 transcripts (IS) increase to >0.1%.
items.reference: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia · ESTABLISHED AND EVOLVING THERAPEUTIC CONCEPTS IN CML (part 3)

items.subtopic: ESTABLISHED AND EVOLVING THERAPEUTIC CONCEPTS IN CML (part 3)
items.title: Approach to TKI Resistance in CML
items.content: Resistance is defined by specific BCR::ABL1 transcript levels after certain durations of therapy. Before switching TKIs, checking for kinase domain mutations is crucial as they guide subsequent treatment choices.
keyPoints: Resistance: BCR::ABL1 transcripts (IS) >10% after 6 months or >1% after 12+ months of frontline therapy.
50% of resistant cases have BCR::ABL1 mutations responsive to specific TKIs.
Y253H, E255K/V, F359V/C/I mutations respond better to dasatinib or bosutinib.
V299L, T315A, F317L/F/I/C mutations respond better to nilotinib.
T315I mutation requires ponatinib or asciminib; consider allogeneic HSCT.
Ponatinib is effective for T315I-mutated CML and after two TKI exposures.
items.reference: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia · ESTABLISHED AND EVOLVING THERAPEUTIC CONCEPTS IN CML (part 3)

items.subtopic: ESTABLISHED AND EVOLVING THERAPEUTIC CONCEPTS IN CML (part 4) | ALLOGENEIC HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELL TRANSPLANT
items.title: Dose Adjustment in Ponatinib Therapy for CML
items.content: Ponatinib therapy, when used at a standard dose of 45 mg daily, is associated with serious side effects. A response-directed dose-adjusted regimen starting at 45 mg and reducing to 15 mg upon achieving MR2 can lower the incidence of arterio-occlusive events.
keyPoints: Ponatinib initial dose: 45 mg daily
Dose reduction to 15 mg after MR2 is achieved
Reduction results in a lower incidence of arterio-occlusive events
items.reference: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia · ESTABLISHED AND EVOLVING THERAPEUTIC CONCEPTS IN CML (part 4) | ALLOGENEIC HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELL TRANSPLANT

items.subtopic: ESTABLISHED AND EVOLVING THERAPEUTIC CONCEPTS IN CML (part 4) | ALLOGENEIC HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELL TRANSPLANT
items.title: Survival Rates with Second-Generation TKIs in CML
items.content: Second-generation TKIs as second-line therapy for chronic-phase CML significantly improve survival rates. The estimated 6- to 8-year survival rates are 65–75%, compared to less than 30–40% before their availability.
keyPoints: Estimated 6- to 8-year survival with second-generation TKIs: 65–75%
Previous survival rates without these therapies: <30–40%
items.reference: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia · ESTABLISHED AND EVOLVING THERAPEUTIC CONCEPTS IN CML (part 4) | ALLOGENEIC HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELL TRANSPLANT

items.subtopic: ESTABLISHED AND EVOLVING THERAPEUTIC CONCEPTS IN CML (part 4) | ALLOGENEIC HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELL TRANSPLANT
items.title: Indications for Allogeneic HSCT in CML
items.content: Allogeneic HSCT is considered a later-line approach in chronic-phase CML, particularly when resistance to second-generation TKIs develops or in T315I-mutated disease. It should be used promptly in blastic phase or accelerated phase to improve outcomes.
keyPoints: Consider allogeneic HSCT for chronic-phase CML with resistance to second-generation TKIs
Use in T315I-mutated disease
Prompt use in blastic or accelerated phase
items.reference: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia · ESTABLISHED AND EVOLVING THERAPEUTIC CONCEPTS IN CML (part 4) | ALLOGENEIC HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELL TRANSPLANT

items.title: Achieving and Monitoring Complete Cytogenetic Response in CML
items.content: In chronic myeloid leukemia (CML), achieving a complete cytogenetic response by 12 months of imatinib therapy is crucial for prolonged survival. If this response is not achieved or if relapse occurs, it indicates treatment failure and necessitates a change in therapy.
keyPoints: Complete cytogenetic response by 12 months with imatinib is essential for prolonged survival.
Failure to achieve complete cytogenetic response by 12 months or later relapse indicates treatment failure.
Patients should be monitored every 6 months with peripheral blood PCR, more frequently if BCR::ABL1 transcript levels are concerning.
Cytogenetic relapse on imatinib necessitates a change in TKI therapy.
Mutational analysis identifies mutations in 30–50% of patients upon cytogenetic relapse.
items.reference: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia · MONITORING THERAPY IN CML | TREATMENT OF ACCELERATED AND BLASTIC PHASES

items.subtopic: TREATMENT OF ACCELERATED AND BLASTIC PHASES
items.title: Treatment Strategies for Accelerated and Blastic Phases in CML
items.content: In the accelerated or blastic phases of CML, second- or third-generation TKIs combined with chemotherapy are preferred to reduce disease burden before allogeneic HSCT. This approach improves response rates and survival.
keyPoints: Second- or third-generation TKIs (dasatinib, nilotinib, bosutinib, ponatinib) are used in accelerated/blastic phases.
Response rates with single-agent TKIs: 30–50% in accelerated phase; 20–30% in blastic phase.
Cytogenetic responses are uncommon (10–30%) and transient in the blastic phase.
Combining TKIs with chemotherapy increases response rates, durability, and survival.
In CML lymphoid blastic phase: Combination therapy results in complete response rates of 70% and median survival times of 3 years.
In nonlymphoid blastic phase: Combination therapy leads to complete remission rates of 30–50% and median survival times of 12 months.
items.reference: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia · MONITORING THERAPY IN CML | TREATMENT OF ACCELERATED AND BLASTIC PHASES

items.subtopic: OTHER TREATMENTS AND SPECIAL THERAPEUTIC CONSIDERATIONS
items.title: Management of Chronic Myeloid Leukemia (CML) During Pregnancy
items.content: In pregnant women with CML, TKI therapy is associated with fetal malformations. Discontinuation of TKIs like imatinib during the first trimester is recommended due to a 2-10% risk of malformations. Hydroxyurea and leukapheresis are alternative management strategies.
keyPoints: TKI therapy in the first trimester is linked with fetal malformations (2–10%).
Imatinib discontinuation during pregnancy resulted in 3 out of 125 babies having malformations.
Dasatinib has a higher reported incidence of malformations (10–12%) compared to imatinib.
Hydroxyurea and leukapheresis are used for managing CML during pregnancy, especially after the first trimester.
Imatinib may be considered safe post-20 weeks gestation.

items.subtopic: OTHER TREATMENTS AND SPECIAL THERAPEUTIC CONSIDERATIONS
items.title: Chemotherapeutic Agents in CML Management
items.content: Hydroxyurea is effective for reducing initial CML burden and can be used with TKIs. Busulfan, due to severe side effects, is rarely used chronically but remains part of HSCT regimens.
keyPoints: Hydroxyurea (0.5–10 g daily) reduces CML burden and sustains responses when combined with TKIs.
Busulfan's side effects include delayed myelosuppression, Addison-like disease, pulmonary/cardiac fibrosis, and myelofibrosis.
Busulfan is now rarely used in chronic management but is part of allogeneic HSCT preparative regimens.
items.reference: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia · OTHER TREATMENTS AND SPECIAL THERAPEUTIC CONSIDERATIONS

items.title: Impact of Early Detection and Cost on CML Outcomes
items.content: In advanced countries, routine screenings lead to early detection of Chronic Myeloid Leukemia (CML), with 50-70% diagnosed incidentally. This contrasts with emerging nations where late-stage diagnosis is common due to symptom-driven evaluations, resulting in worse prognoses despite TKI therapy. The high cost of TKIs ($120,000–270,000 annually) limits accessibility and treatment penetration, affecting survival rates.
keyPoints: 50-70% of CML patients are diagnosed incidentally in advanced countries.
High-risk CML is found in 10% of patients in advanced nations vs. 20-30% in emerging nations.
Annual TKI costs range from $120,000 to $270,000 in the U.S., impacting affordability and treatment penetration.
>85% 10-year survival rate in countries with affordable TKIs (e.g., Sweden).
<50% estimated worldwide 10-year survival rate due to cost barriers.
items.reference: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia · GLOBAL ASPECTS OF CML

items.title: Strategic Use of Generic TKIs and HSCT in CML Management
items.content: The availability of generic imatinib at approximately $500/year makes it a viable frontline therapy for survival. For treatment-free remission (TFR), generic dasatinib is recommended. High costs of later-line therapies have led to considering allogeneic HSCT as third-line, despite its risks and high cost ($20,000–500,000). Effective use of generics can delay the need for HSCT until resistance to second-generation TKIs is evident.
keyPoints: Generic imatinib costs about $500/year, making it a good choice for frontline therapy if survival is the endpoint.
For TFR, generic dasatinib (50 or 100 mg daily) is recommended.
Allogeneic HSCT considered as third-line due to high cost of later-line TKI therapies ($20,000–500,000).
Use of effective generics can delay the need for allogeneic HSCT until resistance to second-generation TKIs occurs.

items.statement: A durable deep molecular response (DMR) for 5+ years is associated with a treatment-free remission rate below 50%.
items.explanation: A durable DMR for 5+ years is associated with a TFR rate of 80% or higher.
items.reference: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia · PROGNOSIS AND CML COURSE

items.statement: Nilotinib is similar in structure to imatinib but is less potent.
items.explanation: Nilotinib is 30 times more potent than imatinib.
items.reference: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia · Chronic Phase Chronic Myeloid Leukemia

items.statement: Dasatinib and bosutinib inhibit the SRC family of kinases in addition to ABLI.
items.explanation: Both dasatinib and bosutinib inhibit the SRC family of kinases in addition to ABL1.
items.reference: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia · Chronic Phase Chronic Myeloid Leukemia

items.subtopic: ESTABLISHED AND EVOLVING THERAPEUTIC CONCEPTS IN CML (part 1)
items.statement: Changing a TKI in patients with good molecular response is currently recommended to achieve complete molecular response.
items.explanation: It is now considered that changing TKI therapy in responding patients may not benefit them and could increase costs and side effects.
items.reference: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia · ESTABLISHED AND EVOLVING THERAPEUTIC CONCEPTS IN CML (part 1)

items.subtopic: ESTABLISHED AND EVOLVING THERAPEUTIC CONCEPTS IN CML (part 1)
items.statement: Generic TKIs have addressed the issue of making therapies available and affordable to all patients.
items.explanation: Making TKI therapies available and affordable is a treatment goal, addressed by the availability of generic TKIs.
items.reference: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia · ESTABLISHED AND EVOLVING THERAPEUTIC CONCEPTS IN CML (part 1)

items.subtopic: ESTABLISHED AND EVOLVING THERAPEUTIC CONCEPTS IN CML (part 2)
items.statement: Second-generation TKIs are associated with higher rates of fluid retention and weight gain compared to imatinib.
items.explanation: Second-generation TKIs are generally associated with lower rates of these adverse events.
items.reference: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia · ESTABLISHED AND EVOLVING THERAPEUTIC CONCEPTS IN CML (part 2)

items.subtopic: ESTABLISHED AND EVOLVING THERAPEUTIC CONCEPTS IN CML (part 2)
items.statement: Ponatinib is associated with a high incidence of systemic hypertension.
items.explanation: Ponatinib has an association with systemic hypertension in 50–60% of cases.
items.reference: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia · ESTABLISHED AND EVOLVING THERAPEUTIC CONCEPTS IN CML (part 2)

items.subtopic: ESTABLISHED AND EVOLVING THERAPEUTIC CONCEPTS IN CML (part 3)
items.statement: TKI cross-intolerance is uncommon due to different chemical structures of TKIs.
items.explanation: TKI cross-intolerance may be more common than previously thought, despite different chemical structures.
items.reference: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia · ESTABLISHED AND EVOLVING THERAPEUTIC CONCEPTS IN CML (part 3)

items.subtopic: ESTABLISHED AND EVOLVING THERAPEUTIC CONCEPTS IN CML (part 3)
items.statement: Ponatinib is indicated for patients with T315I mutations.
items.explanation: Ponatinib is required therapy for T315I-mutated disease.
items.reference: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia · ESTABLISHED AND EVOLVING THERAPEUTIC CONCEPTS IN CML (part 3)

items.subtopic: ESTABLISHED AND EVOLVING THERAPEUTIC CONCEPTS IN CML (part 4) | ALLOGENEIC HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELL TRANSPLANT
items.statement: Allogeneic HSCT is considered a first-line therapy for CML after the year 2000.
items.explanation: Allogeneic HSCT was considered first-line therapy before 2000, but now it's used as a later-line approach due to TKIs.
items.reference: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia · ESTABLISHED AND EVOLVING THERAPEUTIC CONCEPTS IN CML (part 4) | ALLOGENEIC HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELL TRANSPLANT

items.subtopic: ESTABLISHED AND EVOLVING THERAPEUTIC CONCEPTS IN CML (part 4) | ALLOGENEIC HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELL TRANSPLANT
items.statement: Patients with persistent molecular disease after 2 years of TKI therapy have a reasonable 10-year overall survival rate.
items.explanation: Older patients with CML and persistent molecular disease after 2 years of TKI therapy can still have a reasonable 10-year overall survival rate.
items.reference: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia · ESTABLISHED AND EVOLVING THERAPEUTIC CONCEPTS IN CML (part 4) | ALLOGENEIC HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELL TRANSPLANT

items.subtopic: MONITORING THERAPY IN CML | TREATMENT OF ACCELERATED AND BLASTIC PHASES
items.statement: Cytogenetic relapse on imatinib is an indication of treatment failure and necessitates a change in TKI therapy.
items.explanation: Cytogenetic relapse indicates treatment failure, prompting the need to change TKI therapy.
items.reference: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia · MONITORING THERAPY IN CML | TREATMENT OF ACCELERATED AND BLASTIC PHASES

items.subtopic: MONITORING THERAPY IN CML | TREATMENT OF ACCELERATED AND BLASTIC PHASES
items.statement: Changes of TKI therapy for patients with 'slow' molecular response have been proven to provide long-term benefits.
items.explanation: Changes in TKI therapy for 'slow' molecular responses haven't shown long-term benefit compared to changes when more obvious signs of resistance appear.
items.reference: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia · MONITORING THERAPY IN CML | TREATMENT OF ACCELERATED AND BLASTIC PHASES

items.subtopic: OTHER TREATMENTS AND SPECIAL THERAPEUTIC CONSIDERATIONS
items.statement: Hydroxyurea is considered unsafe for use during pregnancy.
items.explanation: Hydroxyurea can be used to manage CML after the first trimester of pregnancy.
items.reference: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia · OTHER TREATMENTS AND SPECIAL THERAPEUTIC CONSIDERATIONS

items.subtopic: OTHER TREATMENTS AND SPECIAL THERAPEUTIC CONSIDERATIONS
items.statement: Interferon $ \alpha $ therapy during pregnancy is completely safe and without side effects.
items.explanation: Interferon $ \alpha $ has side effects that may be troublesome during pregnancy, can be antiangiogenic, and may increase the risk of spontaneous abortions.
items.reference: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia · OTHER TREATMENTS AND SPECIAL THERAPEUTIC CONSIDERATIONS

items.statement: The estimated 10-year survival rate in countries with TKI affordability like Sweden is greater than 85%.
items.explanation: In countries with TKI affordability, the estimated 10-year survival rate is >85%.
items.reference: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia · GLOBAL ASPECTS OF CML

items.statement: The cost of allogeneic HSCT as third-line therapy ranges from $20,000 to $500,000.
items.explanation: Allogeneic HSCT has a one-time cost ranging from $20,000 to $500,000.
items.reference: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia · GLOBAL ASPECTS OF CML

items.subtopic: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia | INCIDENCE AND EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.assertion: The prevalence of chronic myeloid leukemia (CML) is expected to increase.
items.reason: With TKI therapy, the annual mortality rate has decreased significantly.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reduction in mortality due to TKIs explains why prevalence is expected to increase.
items.reference: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia · Chronic Myeloid Leukemia | INCIDENCE AND EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.assertion: Asciminib binds to the ABL1 myristoyl pocket.
items.reason: This binding restores the lost inhibitory activity of the negative regulatory site on ABL1.
items.explanation: Asciminib targets the ABL1 myristoyl pocket, restoring its negative regulatory function.
items.reference: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia · PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 1)

items.assertion: The BCR::ABL1 rearrangement alone is sufficient to cause CML.
items.reason: Experimental models have shown that a second hit is necessary for the development of CML.
items.explanation: While BCR::ABL1 has leukemogenic potential, some models suggest a second hit may be needed.
items.reference: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia · PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 1)

items.assertion: The transition of CML from chronic to accelerated-blastic phase is well understood.
items.reason: Characteristic chromosomal abnormalities and molecular events are associated with disease acceleration.
items.explanation: The transition is poorly understood despite the identification of characteristic chromosomal abnormalities and molecular events.
items.reference: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia · PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 2)

items.assertion: TKIs reduce the transformation rate of CML.
items.reason: TKIs stabilize the CML genome, leading to a reduced transformation rate.
items.explanation: TKIs stabilize the CML genome and thus reduce the transformation rate.
items.reference: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia · PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 2)

items.assertion: High basophil counts in CML can lead to histamine overproduction.
items.reason: Histamine overproduction may cause pruritus, diarrhea, flushing, and gastrointestinal ulcers.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia · CLINICAL PRESENTATION (part 1)

items.assertion: Translocation t(9;22)(q34;p13)/ETV6::ABL1 is classified as a myeloproliferative neoplasm-eosinophilia.
items.reason: This translocation may respond better to second-generation TKIs.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia · CLINICAL PRESENTATION (part 1)

items.assertion: The course of CML has become quite predictable with TKI therapy.
items.reason: Rare sudden transformations are still reported, usually lymphoid blastic transformations that respond to combinations of chemotherapy and TKIs followed by allogeneic HSCT.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason is not the correct explanation for the predictability of CML with TKI therapy.
items.reference: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia · PROGNOSIS AND CML COURSE

items.assertion: Achievement of MMR (MR3) prolongs survival among patients with complete cytogenetic response.
items.reason: MMR is associated with decreased risk of events and CML transformation but not with survival prolongation.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because MMR does not prolong survival, while the reason correctly states its association with decreased risk of events.
items.reference: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia · PROGNOSIS AND CML COURSE

items.reason: Second-generation TKIs include dasatinib, nilotinib, and bosutinib.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia · Chronic Phase Chronic Myeloid Leukemia

items.subtopic: ESTABLISHED AND EVOLVING THERAPEUTIC CONCEPTS IN CML (part 1)
items.assertion: The primary aim in early TKI therapy was to achieve near-normal survival.
items.reason: Survival prolongation was the initial goal, and near-normal survival was an unexpected outcome.
items.explanation: Near-normal survival was not anticipated; the primary aim was to improve survival.
items.reference: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia · ESTABLISHED AND EVOLVING THERAPEUTIC CONCEPTS IN CML (part 1)

items.subtopic: ESTABLISHED AND EVOLVING THERAPEUTIC CONCEPTS IN CML (part 1)
items.assertion: Second-generation TKIs have shown a survival benefit over imatinib.
items.reason: Randomized studies like ENESTnd and DASISION showed better early surrogate endpoints with second-generation TKIs.
items.explanation: Despite better early surrogate endpoints, no study has shown a survival benefit for second-generation TKIs over imatinib.
items.reference: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia · ESTABLISHED AND EVOLVING THERAPEUTIC CONCEPTS IN CML (part 1)

items.subtopic: ESTABLISHED AND EVOLVING THERAPEUTIC CONCEPTS IN CML (part 2)
items.assertion: Imatinib and bosutinib are associated with the lowest incidence of cardiovascular events among TKIs.
items.reason: Both imatinib and bosutinib have lower rates of arterio-occlusive events compared to other TKIs.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, and the reason correctly explains it.
items.reference: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia · ESTABLISHED AND EVOLVING THERAPEUTIC CONCEPTS IN CML (part 2)

items.subtopic: ESTABLISHED AND EVOLVING THERAPEUTIC CONCEPTS IN CML (part 2)
items.assertion: Nilotinib therapy is associated with a high rate of pleural effusions.
items.reason: Dasatinib at higher doses causes more pleural effusions than nilotinib.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; it is dasatinib, not nilotinib, that has a higher rate of pleural effusions.
items.reference: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia · ESTABLISHED AND EVOLVING THERAPEUTIC CONCEPTS IN CML (part 2)

items.subtopic: ESTABLISHED AND EVOLVING THERAPEUTIC CONCEPTS IN CML (part 3)
items.assertion: Patients with TKI resistance often have BCR::ABL1 mutations.
items.reason: About 50% of patients with TKI resistance have BCR::ABL1 mutations that may selectively respond to particular TKIs.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia · ESTABLISHED AND EVOLVING THERAPEUTIC CONCEPTS IN CML (part 3)

items.subtopic: ESTABLISHED AND EVOLVING THERAPEUTIC CONCEPTS IN CML (part 3)
items.assertion: Switching to another second-generation TKI after resistance is highly effective.
items.reason: Switching to another second-generation TKI yields poor results unless specific guiding mutations are present.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because switching to another second-generation TKI generally yields poor results.
items.reference: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia · ESTABLISHED AND EVOLVING THERAPEUTIC CONCEPTS IN CML (part 3)

items.subtopic: ESTABLISHED AND EVOLVING THERAPEUTIC CONCEPTS IN CML (part 4) | ALLOGENEIC HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELL TRANSPLANT
items.assertion: Allogeneic HSCT should be considered for patients in chronic-phase CML who develop resistance to a second-generation TKI.
items.reason: Relying on third-generation TKIs can be very expensive and associated with serious toxicities.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation for considering allogeneic HSCT in these patients.
items.reference: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia · ESTABLISHED AND EVOLVING THERAPEUTIC CONCEPTS IN CML (part 4) | ALLOGENEIC HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELL TRANSPLANT

items.subtopic: ESTABLISHED AND EVOLVING THERAPEUTIC CONCEPTS IN CML (part 4) | ALLOGENEIC HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELL TRANSPLANT
items.assertion: The cure rates with allogeneic HSCT in accelerated-phase CML are ≤20%.
items.reason: Accelerated-phase CML has higher cure rates compared to blastic-phase CML.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because the cure rates for accelerated-phase CML with allogeneic HSCT are 30–50%, not ≤20%.
items.reference: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia · ESTABLISHED AND EVOLVING THERAPEUTIC CONCEPTS IN CML (part 4) | ALLOGENEIC HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELL TRANSPLANT

items.subtopic: MONITORING THERAPY IN CML | TREATMENT OF ACCELERATED AND BLASTIC PHASES
items.assertion: In the blastic phase, combination therapy with TKIs and chemotherapy improves survival.
items.reason: The combination of anti-ALL chemotherapy with TKIs results in higher complete response rates and median survival times compared to historical data.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why combination therapy improves survival.
items.reference: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia · MONITORING THERAPY IN CML | TREATMENT OF ACCELERATED AND BLASTIC PHASES
